2: Food and drink products are responsible for 20-30% of the environmental impacts of private consumption in Europe. After meat and meat products, which have the greatest environmental impact, dairy products are the second important product grouping of this area of consumption. They are responsible for about 5% of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, 10% of eutrophication potential, 5% of acidification and 4% of photochemical ozone crea- tion potential (Tukker et al., 2006).

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) (ISO, 2006a, 2006b) and the envi- ronmental life-cycle-based labels and declarations (ISO, 1999, 2000, 2006c) are suitable tools to enhance competitiveness of dairy sector's companies and encourage a more sustainable consumption at the same time. Many LCA studies have been performed on dairy products (see, for example, the FAO (2010) study on the

quantification of GHG emissions from the world's dairy cattle sector). A great number of them concern milk, whose production is expected to double in the next 40 years, mostly for the change of diet in developing countries (FAO, 2006). Some studies compare conventional and organic milk production in European countries, like Sweden (Cederberg and Mattson, 2000), the Netherlands (Thomassen et al., 2008) and France (van der Werf et al., 2009). Castanheira et al. (2010) evaluate the environmental performance of milk production on a typical Portuguese dairy farm. Penati et al. (2010) investigate the environmental performance of a sample of dairy farms in an Alpine area of Lombardy (Italy). LCA methodology has also been used to assess greenhouse gas emissions (GHG) from Irish (Casey and Holden, 2005) and Swiss milk production system (Nemecek et al., 2011). In New Zealand, an LCA study has been performed on dairy farming aimed at selecting an average national dairy farm (from national statistics) and comparing its eco- efficiency with three contrasting intensification dairy systems (Basset-Mens et al., 2009). A recent LCA study has highlighted considerable differences between the environmental impacts of
Fig. 1. System boundaries of the production of 1 1 of HQ milk.

two milk production systems in the Andean highlands and the coast of Peru (Bartl et al., 2011). All these studies focus on the farming system, i.e. raw milk production at farm, including also crops cultivation and feed production. The whole life cycle of packaged milk has been analyzed in Spain (Hospido et al., 2003), although in a simplified LCA, in Norway (Hogaas Heide, 2002) and in France (Kanyarushoky et al., 2008). An U.S. study (Heller et al., 2008) has evaluated both GHG emissions and energy consump- tion of a large scale organic dairy. A recent review of Yan et al. (2011) has analyzed for comparability thirteen LCA studies of European milk production and has found difficulties for direct comparison due to technical issues, arbitrary choices and incon- sistent assumptions. Type III environmental product declarations (EPD) of the ISO 14025 standard (ISO, 2006c) could contribute to improve comparability of LCA studies thanks to the use of Product Category Rules (PCR). Besides the definition and description of the product category, a PCR document provides instructions about the choice of functional unit, system boundaries and impact categories, and sets data quality requirements, allocation rules and data collection methods that have to be followed in the LCA study at the basis of the environmental declarations of the products of a specific category.
After the development of a PCR document for milk and milk based liquid products (Di Stefano, 2006), in 2007 the first EPD on milk was published according to the International EPD System.

The present study on high quality (HQ) milk2 was aimed at identifying critical aspect affecting the comparability of EPDs in the food sector and was therefore carried out in compliance with the PCR for milk prepared according to the International EPD System.
The activity was carried out in collaboration with a large Italian retailing company, Coop Italia, and in the framework of an Agree- ment Protocol with the Italian Ministry of Environment.

Section 2 of this paper includes the description of goal, scope and data collection of the LCA study performed in agreement with the PCR for milk (Di Stefano, 2006). Section 3 reports on the results of this study and the comparison with the EPD registered. After the publication of the updated version of the PCR (Di Stefano, 2010), the LCA study was revised for the part of the inventory concerning methane emissions from cattle and emis- sions due to manure management and spreading. Main results of the revised study are discussed in Section 4 and Section 5 briefly discusses comparison with other published studies of LCA on milk.

13: <header>2.2. Decision context, functional unit and system boundaries </header>
The decision context of the study is the archetype A as defined in the ILCD Handbook, therefore an attributional approach was applied (European Commission - JRC IES, 2010).

Different choices for the definition of the functional unit (FU) for milk production can be found in literature: FU definition can be based on mass, i.e. 1 kg or 1 metric tonne (Basset-Mens et al., 2009; Castanheira et al., 2010) or on volume, i.e. 1 1 or 1000 1 (Hospido et al., 2003; Hogaas Heide, 2002). In the PCRs for food products, Schau and Fet (2008) recommend to use a quality cor- rected FU, which considers the nutrient content of the product as well as its mass. Indeed, some studies use 1 kg Energy Corrected Milk (ECM), or 1 kg Fat and Protein Corrected Milk (FPCM), which take into account both the fat and protein content of milk (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; van der Werf et al., 2009; Thomassen et al., 2008; Casey and Holden, 2005). Anyway, the PCR document for milk defines the FU as 1 1 of packaged milk, therefore 1 1 of HQ milk bottled in a Tetra Top package was considered in this study.
Milk production includes the following phases (Fig. 1):

<list>Raw milk production at farm and transport to dairies; 
Pasteurization; 
Tetra Top R production; 
Filling and packaging; 
Delivery to distribution centres.</list>

System boundaries of this study comprise all the phases above, including crops cultivation for fodder production and direct emissions on farm. In particular, the following items were included: production and transport of purchased feed and complementary fodder produced outside the farms; production and transport of fertilizers, pesticides and detergents used at farms and dairies; production and use of primary and secondary packaging of milk (cardboard, polyethylene films and wooden pallets); disposal and treatment of waste produced in each phase. Buildings, infrastructures and equipments were not included in the system boundaries, but they were included in some Ecoinvent processes (see Section 2.4). The production of medicines, and the consumer's and post-use phases were not included as well.
Milk production at farms is a multifunctional process, as milk and meat (from both calves and cows slaughtered) are produced at the same time. So, it is important to determine to what extent the

environmental impacts of these processes should be allocated to the product investigated. Cederberg and Stadig (2003) use system expansion to include milk and beef production systems and compare the results obtained to the results obtained using economic allocation. Other LCA studies on milk production perform an economic allocation between milk and meat (Hospido et al., 2003; Thomassen et al., 2008), use a mass allocation (Casey and Holden, 2005) or a biological allocation, i.e. the causality between the amount and quality of the feed given to dairy cows and the two outputs milk and meat (Cederberg and Mattson, 2000; Hogaas Heide, 2002). Recently a study has been published on the influ- ence that allocation choices have on the final results of LCA and carbon footprint of milk (Flysjö et al., 2011). In the present study, in agreement with the goals set and with the PCR for milk, a conser- vative approach was adopted and all impacts were allocated to milk production.

17: <header>2.3. Impact categories </header>
According to the PCR for milk the following impact categories and parameters were analyzed ³:

<list>Global warming (IPCC, 2001); 
Ozone depletion (Solomon and Albritton, 1992); 
Photochemical Oxidation (Heijungs et al., 1992; Andersson- Sköld et al., 1992; Jenkin and Hayman, 1999; Derwent et al., 1998; Guinée et al., 2002); 
Acidification (Guinée et al., 2002; Huijbregts, 1999); 
Eutrophication (Guinée et al., 2002); 
Resources with energy content (renewable and non renewable sources); 
Resources without energy content; 
Hazardous and non hazardous waste.</list>

As highlighted by Yan et al. (2011) the assessment of the toxicity is often excluded in published studies of LCA of milk, due to limited availability of data. Even if we recognise that this is an important environmental aspect to consider in LCA studies on food products, we did not collect/estimate data about emissions due to pesticides use because the goal of the study was focused on the comparison with the registered EPD, which does not include the assessment of toxicity impacts. Moreover, a recommended method still misses (Finnveden et al., 2009). Also land use is excluded from the list of the impact categories of the PCR for milk. This category is important for the assessment of agricultural products, but it is still under development and, despite the numerous existing proposals, consensus on methods with readily-available lists of intervention- characterisation factor combinations is missing (Bare, 2010).

Acidification (Guinée et al., 2002; Huijbregts, 1999); 
Eutrophication (Guinée et al., 2002); 
Resources with energy content (renewable and non renewable sources); 
Resources without energy content; 
Hazardous and non hazardous waste.</list>

As highlighted by Yan et al. (2011) the assessment of the toxicity is often excluded in published studies of LCA of milk, due to limited availability of data. Even if we recognise that this is an important environmental aspect to consider in LCA studies on food products, we did not collect/estimate data about emissions due to pesticides use because the goal of the study was focused on the comparison with the registered EPD, which does not include the assessment of toxicity impacts. Moreover, a recommended method still misses (Finnveden et al., 2009). Also land use is excluded from the list of the impact categories of the PCR for milk. This category is important for the assessment of agricultural products, but it is still under development and, despite the numerous existing proposals, consensus on methods with readily-available lists of intervention- characterisation factor combinations is missing (Bare, 2010).
19: The brand of HQ milk object of this study is produced by two dairy factories, A and B. Dairy A collects raw milk from 5 farms, dairy B from 13 farms. About 50% of their milk goes to the production of this brand of HQ. Milk suppliers of dairy B, which are located in a mountain region, have a yearly average production lower than suppliers of dairy A. A representative sample was created by dividing farms in classes (small, medium, large), the size of which was defined according to the milk daily production

(Table 1). The choice of this grouping criterion was based on experts' assumption that management practices at farms depend on the geographical area and the farm size. In this way, three farms for each dairy (A1, A2, A3, B1, B2, B3) were selected as representative of the whole production system and involved in the data collection. The sample covered the 60% of the entire production of raw milk. Remaining farms were classified according to their daily average production and assumed to be managed as the sample farm of their class. To calculate the inventory of of HQ milk, data were weighted by the milk production volume of the farms.

23: Farms cultivate different crops for fodder production. For each farm data were collected about hectares cultivated, amount of seeds used, consumption of chemical fertilizers and pesticides (the latter only used for maize cultivation), crops yearly yield, and consumption of diesel for tractors. Emissions generated by diesel use were estimated in agreement with data of Ecoinvent for agri- cultural field work processes (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). For each cowshed information was collected about number of dairy cows, lactating and dry, heifers and calves and yearly milk production (Table 1). Cows feed consists both of forage produced within farms (maize and barley silage, maize and barley grains, rye- grass silage, hay) and of purchased feed, like complementary fodder or straw, cotton and soy seeds. As every farm has a specific diet and daily intake for each type of animal, data about quantity and type of feed, including complementary fodder, were collected. Primary data on both electricity and LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) consumption were collected at each farm, and the environmental burdens associated with their production and use were considered. Waste produced at farms mainly consists of plastic and nylon nets, oils, packaging (i.e. cardboard, paper and glasses) and sanitary waste. As PCR state that "specific data shall be collected about waste produced", primary data were collected on the amount and destination of waste produced, and Ecoinvent database was used for modelling the waste treatments. 26: After milking process and storage at farms, raw milk is trans- ported to dairies, where it is controlled in the quality control laboratories. In 2008 dairy A manufactured 85% of the overall production of the brand of HQ milk investigated, dairy B 15%. The total production of HQ milk was 7.7 million litres. Both dairies produced also other types of milk. Data on yearly electricity and methane consumption were collected at each dairy and allocated on mass on the basis of the yearly overall milk production and the yearly production of product investigated (Table 2).

At dairies milk undergoes a pasteurization process, which starts with preheating to 51 °C and skimming, which removes impurities and particles and brings the milk to the required fat content. In the second step, milk is heated to 74-77 °C for 15 S, cooled down to and then stored. The following phase is milk packaging in Tetra Top® bottles, produced directly at dairies and made of paperboard with an injection moulded plastic top. After filling, Tetra Top® bottles are capped, packed in cardboard boxes and in pallets, and wrapped with polyethylene films. Primary data were collected about the amount of cardboard and polyethylene film used for primary and secondary packaging. Finally, packaged milk is stored

in refrigerated cells, which use R404, R22 or glycol as cooling fluids, and delivered to distribution centres located throughout Italy. Primary data were collected about quantity of milk transported to dairies and to distribution centres, transport distances and type of trucks used for each transport.

Trucks, storage silos as well as pasteurization plants are cleaned by Cleaning in Place Systems, which consist of several hot and cool water rinses using also sodium hydroxide and nitric acid. The environmental burdens associated with their production and with the production of other detergents used for disinfection and cleaning maintenance of the plants and facilities were assessed. Data on water consumption used at dairies for pasteurization process, cleaning and sanitizing were collected as well.

Data were collected on the amount of and destination of waste produced at dairies, mainly plastic, oils, cardboard and paper. A wastewater treatment plant operates at dairy B, whereas waste- water produced at dairy A is directly discharged into municipal wastewater treatment plant. Secondary data from Ecoinvent were used for modelling waste and wastewater treatments.
To calculate the inventory of of HQ milk, data were weighted by the HQ milk production volume of the dairies.

29: <header>3.1. Impact assessment results of high quality milk production </header>

Milk life cycle was divided into four main phases: 1) farm operation (i.e. agricultural phase and cows breeding and milking); 2) milk delivery to dairy (i.e. transport from farm to dairy); 3) milk processing at dairies; 4) transport to distribution centres. The results are shown in Tables 3 and 4.

The 'farm operation' phase dominates the milk production chain in all impact categories, with its contribution ranging from 61% (Ozone layer depletion) to 97% (Eutrophication). The normalization results, based on the method CML2001, World 1990 (Guinée et al., 2002), show that the contribution of ozone depletion and photochemical oxidation to the environmental impacts is negligible (Fig. 2).

The contribution of the farm operation phase to total global warming potential (1.3 kg CO2 eq.) is due to CH4 (43%), N20 (33%) and CO2 (23%). They are mainly emitted from cows and heifers, chemical and organic fertilizers use, and diesel consumption during agricultural work processes, respectively. Transport accounts for 4% of the total results. The contribution of milk processing at dairies is 0.17 kg CO2 eq.: the 97% is due to CO2 emissions and the 2% is due to N20 airborne emissions. They are mainly emitted from energy consumption and production of Tetra Top bottles and from production of nitric acid, used in cleaning systems, respectively. The emissions of CH4, CO2 and N20 account for 37%, 34% and 29% respectively of total global warming potential.

For Acidification, about 9.1E-03 kg SO2 eq. come from farm operation phase: NH3, mainly emitted from organic fertilizers applied to the soils (i.e. manure and slurry), gives a contribution equal to 78%; NO emissions, mainly due to diesel consumption,

5: <header>3.2. Comparison with the registered EPD of milk </header>

This LCA study (here called M1) was compared with the first version of EPD of HQ milk published in 2007 (here called M2)4. Both M1 and M2 studies had the same system boundaries, i.e. from cradle to final distribution, and were carried out in compliance with the PCR for milk (Di Stefano, 2006), but different hypotheses were assumed during the modelling phase because detailed rules were missing. In cooperation with the authors of the study M2 (Fantin et al., 2010), we investigated to what extent the assumptions affect the results of the studies.

The inclusion of fertilizers' field emissions is a critical point for the comparability of the studies. According to the version 2006 of the PCR, LCA practitioners decide whether to include them or not, and, in affirmative case, they have to choose substances and

emission models. In study M2, NO, phosphorus and potassium emissions into water from fertilizers use were inventoried, but N20 and NH3 airborne emissions were not. Different models to estimate emissions were used in M1 and M2.
Lack of detailed rules in PCR led also to include in study M1 the production of complementary fodder and the waste treatment processes, which were not included in M2.

The comparison between the results of M1 and M2 is hard to analyze. For global warming the difference is only 18%, but the inclusion of N20 air emissions accounts for about one third of the total results of study M1. For acidification, the difference between the two studies is more than 80%, due to the choice of including or not NH3 air emissions from fertilizers use. Eutrophication value in study M1 is 76% higher than in study M2, but the inclusion of NH3 emissions is not enough to justify the differences, which are likely due to the emissions dispersion models. The category resource with energy content has a value one order of magnitude higher in study M1 than in study M2, mainly due to the inclusion of the biomass (soy, maize, barley) energy content. The category resource without energy content has a value two orders of magnitude higher in study M1 than in study M2. This difference mainly depends on sodium chloride and calcium carbonate flows contained in complementary fodder, which was not included in study M2, and on gravel, which is included in the inventory of waste treatment and of transport in study M1. Finally, waste production has a value one order of magnitude lower in study M1 than in study M2, because in M1 some data about waste production at dairies were incomplete.

On the basis of this analysis, which has highlighted some weak points of the PCR document, comments were sent to the PCR moderator during the open consultation of the revised version (Di Stefano, 2010), aiming to limit the need for discretionary choices by LCA practitioners and to improve the EPDs comparability.

Another study, here called M3, was carried out aimed at addressing two weak points of study M1: data on methane emis- sions from cattle, and emissions due to manure management and spreading. The study was carried out in agreement with the revised PCR document (Di Stefano, 2010). This document provides more detailed instructions if compared with the first version, in

particular it explicitly requires the inclusion of fertilizers' field emissions and enteric fermentation. Nevertheless, it gives LCA practitioners the freedom of choosing specific models for calcula- tion of these emissions.

Methane emissions at farms are mainly due to enteric fermen- tation from dairy cows and to manure management (handling and storage of manure). In study M1 the reference value of 130 kg CH4 per head per year suggested by the first PCR was used, but it was unclear whether this value should include both enteric fermenta- tion and manure management. Data published by Castanheira et al. (2010), who use the Tier 1 method proposed by IPCC (2006), suggest that the annual value of 130 kg per cow could include both enteric fermentation and manure management for dairy cows, but it would overestimate emissions from heifers, leading thus to a conservative approach of the study. In study M3, to calculate methane emissions at farms we referred to the inventory of Italian greenhouse gas emissions between the years 1990 and 2008 pub- lished by ISPRA (2010). In 2008 in Italy annual methane emissions from enteric fermentation were 114.7 kg per dairy cow and 45.5 kg per heifer and calf; emissions from manure management were 15 kg and 7.7 kg respectively.

Direct and indirect N20 airborne emissions, NH3 airborne emissions and NO3 waterborne emissions due to manure management and spreading and to chemical fertilizers use were estimated according to IPCC (2006). Direct N20 emissions occurring during manure management are produced by nitrification and denitrification of nitrogen contained in manure. Calculation was based on the total amount of nitrogen excreted by dairy cattle living at farms (daily excretion rates equal to 0.48 kg N/1000 kg animal mass for dairy cows and 0.33 kg/1000 kg animal mass for heifers and calves), the typical animal mass (600 kg for dairy cows, 400 kg for heifers and 230 kg for calves) and the emission factor for the manure management system of the farms sampled (0.005 kg N20-N per kg N excreted for manure stored in tanks as a mixture of liquid and solid dejections). Indirect N20 emissions from manure management were calculated assuming that 40% of the manure nitrogen is volatilized as NH3 and NO and multiplying this value by the emission factor for N20 emissions from atmospheric deposition of nitrogen on soils and water surfaces (0.01 kg N20-N per kg NH3-N
volatilized). NH3-N emissions were estimated equal to 40% of the nitrogen excreted, in agreement with Castanheira et al. (2010).

Direct N20 emissions resulting from the application to soils of nitrogen contained in both chemical fertilizers and manure were calculated considering that 1% of the total nitrogen applied is released as N20 (IPCC (2006)). Nitrogen content in manure, esti- mated on the basis of the nitrogen excreted and volatilized and of the amount of manure produced by each farm, was in a range between 2 and 6 kg N per tonne of manure (from 30 to 170 kg N ha-1. Indirect N20 emissions occur through two path- ways (IPCC (2006)): (1) volatilization of nitrogen as NH3 and NOx, which was estimated equal to 10% and 20% of the amount of nitrogen contained in chemical fertilizers and manure respec- tively (Castanheira et al., 2010), followed by deposition of nitrogen onto soil/water; (2) leaching and runoff of nitrogen (0.3 kg NO3-N per kg N applied) from chemical fertilizers and manure. In both pathways initrification/denitrification reactions follow and produce N20 emissions, which can be estimated as 0.01 kg N20-N emitted per kg N volatilized and 0.0075 kg N20-N per kg N leaching/runoff. NH3 airborne emissions and NO3 waterborne emissions were quantified in agreement with the above mentioned rates of volatilization and leaching/runoff respectively. Phosphorus waterborne emissions from chemical fertilizers and manure spreading were calculated according to the dispersion models of Ecoinvent (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007), considering the phosphorus content in manure equal to 0.2% (Regione Emilia Romagna, 2009).
Table 5 summarises the impact assessment results of study M3. The 'farm operation' phase always dominates the milk produc- tion chain in all impact categories.

A more accurate evaluation of the CH4 and N20 emissions gives lower values of global warming potential if compared to study M1. The farm operation stage is responsible for 1.06 kg CO2 eq., due to emissions of CH4 (45%), N20 (30%) and CO2 (25%). CH4 is emitted mainly from enteric fermentation and manure management; N20 emissions are mainly due to chemical fertilizers use and manure management and use.
The results of acidification have increased: 2.1E-02 kg SO2 eq. are attributed to farm operation phase, mainly due to NH3

Another study, here called M3, was carried out aimed at addressing two weak points of study M1: data on methane emis- sions from cattle, and emissions due to manure management and spreading. The study was carried out in agreement with the revised PCR document (Di Stefano, 2010). This document provides more detailed instructions if compared with the first version, in

particular it explicitly requires the inclusion of fertilizers' field emissions and enteric fermentation. Nevertheless, it gives LCA practitioners the freedom of choosing specific models for calcula- tion of these emissions.

Methane emissions at farms are mainly due to enteric fermen- tation from dairy cows and to manure management (handling and storage of manure). In study M1 the reference value of 130 kg CH4 per head per year suggested by the first PCR was used, but it was unclear whether this value should include both enteric fermenta- tion and manure management. Data published by Castanheira et al. (2010), who use the Tier 1 method proposed by IPCC (2006), suggest that the annual value of 130 kg per cow could include both enteric fermentation and manure management for dairy cows, but it would overestimate emissions from heifers, leading thus to a conservative approach of the study. In study M3, to calculate methane emissions at farms we referred to the inventory of Italian greenhouse gas emissions between the years 1990 and 2008 pub- lished by ISPRA (2010). In 2008 in Italy annual methane emissions from enteric fermentation were 114.7 kg per dairy cow and 45.5 kg per heifer and calf; emissions from manure management were 15 kg and 7.7 kg respectively.

Direct and indirect N20 airborne emissions, NH3 airborne emissions and NO3 waterborne emissions due to manure management and spreading and to chemical fertilizers use were estimated according to IPCC (2006). Direct N20 emissions occurring during manure management are produced by nitrification and denitrification of nitrogen contained in manure. Calculation was based on the total amount of nitrogen excreted by dairy cattle living at farms (daily excretion rates equal to 0.48 kg N/1000 kg animal mass for dairy cows and 0.33 kg/1000 kg animal mass for heifers and calves), the typical animal mass (600 kg for dairy cows, 400 kg for heifers and 230 kg for calves) and the emission factor for the manure management system of the farms sampled (0.005 kg N20-N per kg N excreted for manure stored in tanks as a mixture of liquid and solid dejections). Indirect N20 emissions from manure management were calculated assuming that 40% of the manure nitrogen is volatilized as NH3 and NO and multiplying this value by the emission factor for N20 emissions from atmospheric deposition of nitrogen on soils and water surfaces (0.01 kg N20-N per kg NH3-N
volatilized). NH3-N emissions were estimated equal to 40% of the nitrogen excreted, in agreement with Castanheira et al. (2010).

Direct N20 emissions resulting from the application to soils of nitrogen contained in both chemical fertilizers and manure were calculated considering that 1% of the total nitrogen applied is released as N20 (IPCC (2006)). Nitrogen content in manure, esti- mated on the basis of the nitrogen excreted and volatilized and of the amount of manure produced by each farm, was in a range between 2 and 6 kg N per tonne of manure (from 30 to 170 kg N ha-1. Indirect N20 emissions occur through two path- ways (IPCC (2006)): (1) volatilization of nitrogen as NH3 and NOx, which was estimated equal to 10% and 20% of the amount of nitrogen contained in chemical fertilizers and manure respec- tively (Castanheira et al., 2010), followed by deposition of nitrogen onto soil/water; (2) leaching and runoff of nitrogen (0.3 kg NO3-N per kg N applied) from chemical fertilizers and manure. In both pathways initrification/denitrification reactions follow and produce N20 emissions, which can be estimated as 0.01 kg N20-N emitted per kg N volatilized and 0.0075 kg N20-N per kg N leaching/runoff. NH3 airborne emissions and NO3 waterborne emissions were quantified in agreement with the above mentioned rates of volatilization and leaching/runoff respectively. Phosphorus waterborne emissions from chemical fertilizers and manure spreading were calculated according to the dispersion models of Ecoinvent (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007), considering the phosphorus content in manure equal to 0.2% (Regione Emilia Romagna, 2009).
Table 5 summarises the impact assessment results of study M3. The 'farm operation' phase always dominates the milk produc- tion chain in all impact categories.

A more accurate evaluation of the CH4 and N20 emissions gives lower values of global warming potential if compared to study M1. The farm operation stage is responsible for 1.06 kg CO2 eq., due to emissions of CH4 (45%), N20 (30%) and CO2 (25%). CH4 is emitted mainly from enteric fermentation and manure management; N20 emissions are mainly due to chemical fertilizers use and manure management and use.
The results of acidification have increased: 2.1E-02 kg SO2 eq. are attributed to farm operation phase, mainly due to NH3

particular it explicitly requires the inclusion of fertilizers' field emissions and enteric fermentation. Nevertheless, it gives LCA practitioners the freedom of choosing specific models for calcula- tion of these emissions.

Methane emissions at farms are mainly due to enteric fermen- tation from dairy cows and to manure management (handling and storage of manure). In study M1 the reference value of 130 kg CH4 per head per year suggested by the first PCR was used, but it was unclear whether this value should include both enteric fermenta- tion and manure management. Data published by Castanheira et al. (2010), who use the Tier 1 method proposed by IPCC (2006), suggest that the annual value of 130 kg per cow could include both enteric fermentation and manure management for dairy cows, but it would overestimate emissions from heifers, leading thus to a conservative approach of the study. In study M3, to calculate methane emissions at farms we referred to the inventory of Italian greenhouse gas emissions between the years 1990 and 2008 pub- lished by ISPRA (2010). In 2008 in Italy annual methane emissions from enteric fermentation were 114.7 kg per dairy cow and 45.5 kg per heifer and calf; emissions from manure management were 15 kg and 7.7 kg respectively.

Direct and indirect N20 airborne emissions, NH3 airborne emissions and NO3 waterborne emissions due to manure management and spreading and to chemical fertilizers use were estimated according to IPCC (2006). Direct N20 emissions occurring during manure management are produced by nitrification and denitrification of nitrogen contained in manure. Calculation was based on the total amount of nitrogen excreted by dairy cattle living at farms (daily excretion rates equal to 0.48 kg N/1000 kg animal mass for dairy cows and 0.33 kg/1000 kg animal mass for heifers and calves), the typical animal mass (600 kg for dairy cows, 400 kg for heifers and 230 kg for calves) and the emission factor for the manure management system of the farms sampled (0.005 kg N20-N per kg N excreted for manure stored in tanks as a mixture of liquid and solid dejections). Indirect N20 emissions from manure management were calculated assuming that 40% of the manure nitrogen is volatilized as NH3 and NO and multiplying this value by the emission factor for N20 emissions from atmospheric deposition of nitrogen on soils and water surfaces (0.01 kg N20-N per kg NH3-N
volatilized). NH3-N emissions were estimated equal to 40% of the nitrogen excreted, in agreement with Castanheira et al. (2010).

Direct N20 emissions resulting from the application to soils of nitrogen contained in both chemical fertilizers and manure were calculated considering that 1% of the total nitrogen applied is released as N20 (IPCC (2006)). Nitrogen content in manure, esti- mated on the basis of the nitrogen excreted and volatilized and of the amount of manure produced by each farm, was in a range between 2 and 6 kg N per tonne of manure (from 30 to 170 kg N ha-1. Indirect N20 emissions occur through two path- ways (IPCC (2006)): (1) volatilization of nitrogen as NH3 and NOx, which was estimated equal to 10% and 20% of the amount of nitrogen contained in chemical fertilizers and manure respec- tively (Castanheira et al., 2010), followed by deposition of nitrogen onto soil/water; (2) leaching and runoff of nitrogen (0.3 kg NO3-N per kg N applied) from chemical fertilizers and manure. In both pathways initrification/denitrification reactions follow and produce N20 emissions, which can be estimated as 0.01 kg N20-N emitted per kg N volatilized and 0.0075 kg N20-N per kg N leaching/runoff. NH3 airborne emissions and NO3 waterborne emissions were quantified in agreement with the above mentioned rates of volatilization and leaching/runoff respectively. Phosphorus waterborne emissions from chemical fertilizers and manure spreading were calculated according to the dispersion models of Ecoinvent (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007), considering the phosphorus content in manure equal to 0.2% (Regione Emilia Romagna, 2009).
Table 5 summarises the impact assessment results of study M3. The 'farm operation' phase always dominates the milk produc- tion chain in all impact categories.

A more accurate evaluation of the CH4 and N20 emissions gives lower values of global warming potential if compared to study M1. The farm operation stage is responsible for 1.06 kg CO2 eq., due to emissions of CH4 (45%), N20 (30%) and CO2 (25%). CH4 is emitted mainly from enteric fermentation and manure management; N20 emissions are mainly due to chemical fertilizers use and manure management and use.

18: The results of global warming, acidification and eutrophication obtained in study M3-both raw milk production at farm gate and the entire life cycle-were compared with the results of other pub- lished studies (Table 6).

Raw milk production at farms is confirmed to be the main envi- ronmental hotspot. This phase accounts for 80% of global warming in Hospido et al. (2003), 88% in Kanyarushoki et al. (2008) and 68% in Heller et al. (2008). Castanheira et al. (2010) show that the contri- bution of CH4, CO2 and N20 to total global warming at farms are 64%, 19% and 17% respectively. CH4 emissions are mainly due to enteric fermentation and manure management, N20 to the production of fodder and concentrates. Thomassen et al. (2008) conclude that for the conventional farm system in the Netherlands the contribution to total global warming at farms is 34% for CH4, 38% for N20, and 29% for CO2. Heller et al. (2008) report for CH4 a contribution of 33% to the whole milk life cycle. Bartl et al. (2011) show very high global warming results in the highland system, due to low milk production per cow (<3 kg cow day-1 and high methane emissions from enteric fermentation. Farm operation phase accounts for 59% of acidification in Hospido et al. (2003) and for 92% in Kanyarushoki et al. (2008). Castanheira et al. (2010) conclude that NH3 emissions are responsible for 87% of total acidification at farms and are mainly

caused by emissions resulting from volatilization of the nitrogen contained in manure and fertilisers applied to soils. Similar conclusions are reached by Cederberg and Mattson (2000) (contri- bution of NH3 equal to 90%), by Thomassen et al. (2008) (81%) and by Bartl et al. (2011) (98% and 76% of total acidification in the highland and coastal systems respectively). Finally, farm operation phase accounts for 95% of eutrophication in Kanyarushoki et al. (2008) and for 74% in Hospido et al. (2003). NH3 emissions from manure management and NO3 emissions from the application of fertilizers and manure to soils are major contributors in Portuguese farms (Castanheira et al., 2010). Thomassen et al. (2008) find that leaching of both NO3 and - and volatilized NH3 accounts for 32%, 53% and 12% respectively in conventional farms. Bartl et al. (2011) show a contribution of NO3 emissions equal to 60% and 63% in the high- land and coastal systems respectively.

The results of study M3 fall within the literature range of values. Flows that mainly affect the results are the same, but their contri- bution to the total results is different due to assumptions and models used in each study. In particular, the estimation of emis- sions from manure management and from fertilizers use is a critical aspect and strongly affects the final results.

22: This study on HQ milk highlights some critical aspects affecting the comparability of LCA studies and EPDs of the food sector. It was performed in two steps. In the first step (M1), the version 2006 of the PCR for milk of the International EPD Systems was adopted and the results were compared with the published EPD of another brand of HQ milk (M2). Very large differences were identified between the two studies. Though differences do exist between the production processes of the two brands compared, they are considered by sectoral experts not so large to explain the differ- ences of the results: about 80% in acidification and eutrophication, one order of magnitude for resource with energy content and two

orders of magnitude in resource without energy content. Indeed, the most relevant source of differences between the studies is the choice of different system models, mainly due to lack of detailed provisions in the PCR, especially for: fertilizers field emissions and choice of the models for their estimation; inclusion of the complementary fodder production; waste management. The results of this analysis were used in the open consultation of the PCR revised version to highlight the importance of including more detailed instructions.

In the second step, the LCA (M3) was carried out in agreement with the revised version of the PCR. Although that document provides more detailed instructions, it does not specify the models for calculation of enteric methane emissions and of emissions from manure management. The main difference between M1 and M3 is the introduction of more sophisticated models for the estimation of those emissions. The comparison shows that more sophisticated models estimate lower values of global warming potential, due to a more accurate estimation of the enteric emissions, and higher values of acidification, due to a conservative calculation of NH3 emissions from manure management, not included in M1. These findings confirm that, in lack of primary data the choice of the models for calculation of emissions in agriculture is a critical aspect that strongly affects the results of LCA studies and should be more carefully considered at the moment of producing PCR for agricul- tural products.

In both M1 and M3, raw milk production at farms dominates the whole life cycle for all impact categories (its percentage contribu- tion is always higher than 60%). Main flows affecting the results are: CH4 from enteric fermentation, N20 and NH3 airborne emissions, and NO3 waterborne emissions coming from manure management and fertilizers use, and CO2 from diesel consumption. As a conse- quence, emission mitigation strategies should be mainly focused on these flows and could include, for example, the selection of a diet for reducing enteric emissions, the introduction of energy recovery from anaerobic digestion of manure, and the optimisation of the use of fertilizers by adopting precision farming techniques (Weiss and Baret, 2000; Tian et al., 2007).

Further improvements of the study might be obtained from a more accurate calculation of enteric methane emissions based on diet characteristics (Brogna, 2011) and of NH3 field emissions. Moreover, the assessment of toxicity and land use could be included, going beyond the instructions of the PCR.

The comparison of M3 with some published LCA studies on milk shows that the results are within the literature range of values, though large differences exist in modelling choices (FU, system boundaries, allocation, etc.). Comparability of the studies is also affected by the use of different models/assumptions for emissions estimation and sources of secondary data: indeed the inventory results of a process might differ because the process is modelled in a different way and not because it is actually different.

In general, the availability of public sources of reference back- ground data (see, for example, the initiative of the European Plat- form on LCA5), an improved methodological harmonisation (European Commission - JRC IES, 2010) and the achievement of sectoral guidance (Katajajuuri et al., 2010; Masoni and Zamagni, 2011) are key aspects to improve LCA studies' comparability. In addition, specifically for agricultural products, where primary data in environmental hotspots of the life cycle (e.g. emissions from manure management and fertilisers use) are seldom available, these aspects should be carefully addressed and sensitivity to the
models/assumptions and uncertainty of the results should be evaluated (Yan et al., 2011).

The results of this analysis were used in the open consultation of the PCR revised version to highlight the importance of including more detailed instructions.

In the second step, the LCA (M3) was carried out in agreement with the revised version of the PCR. Although that document provides more detailed instructions, it does not specify the models for calculation of enteric methane emissions and of emissions from manure management. The main difference between M1 and M3 is the introduction of more sophisticated models for the estimation of those emissions. The comparison shows that more sophisticated models estimate lower values of global warming potential, due to a more accurate estimation of the enteric emissions, and higher values of acidification, due to a conservative calculation of NH3 emissions from manure management, not included in M1. These findings confirm that, in lack of primary data the choice of the models for calculation of emissions in agriculture is a critical aspect that strongly affects the results of LCA studies and should be more carefully considered at the moment of producing PCR for agricul- tural products.

In both M1 and M3, raw milk production at farms dominates the whole life cycle for all impact categories (its percentage contribu- tion is always higher than 60%). Main flows affecting the results are: CH4 from enteric fermentation, N20 and NH3 airborne emissions, and NO3 waterborne emissions coming from manure management and fertilizers use, and CO2 from diesel consumption. As a conse- quence, emission mitigation strategies should be mainly focused on these flows and could include, for example, the selection of a diet for reducing enteric emissions, the introduction of energy recovery from anaerobic digestion of manure, and the optimisation of the use of fertilizers by adopting precision farming techniques (Weiss and Baret, 2000; Tian et al., 2007).

Further improvements of the study might be obtained from a more accurate calculation of enteric methane emissions based on diet characteristics (Brogna, 2011) and of NH3 field emissions. Moreover, the assessment of toxicity and land use could be included, going beyond the instructions of the PCR.

The comparison of M3 with some published LCA studies on milk shows that the results are within the literature range of values, though large differences exist in modelling choices (FU, system boundaries, allocation, etc.). Comparability of the studies is also affected by the use of different models/assumptions for emissions estimation and sources of secondary data: indeed the inventory results of a process might differ because the process is modelled in a different way and not because it is actually different.

In general, the availability of public sources of reference back- ground data (see, for example, the initiative of the European Plat- form on LCA5), an improved methodological harmonisation (European Commission - JRC IES, 2010) and the achievement of sectoral guidance (Katajajuuri et al., 2010; Masoni and Zamagni, 2011) are key aspects to improve LCA studies' comparability. In addition, specifically for agricultural products, where primary data in environmental hotspots of the life cycle (e.g. emissions from manure management and fertilisers use) are seldom available, these aspects should be carefully addressed and sensitivity to the
models/assumptions and uncertainty of the results should be evaluated (Yan et al., 2011).

In the first step (M1), the version 2006 of the PCR for milk of the International EPD Systems was adopted and the results were compared with the published EPD of another brand of HQ milk (M2). Very large differences were identified between the two studies.
